architecture.summary: Sparse autoencoders (SAEs) decompose dense language model embeddings into sparse activation vectors weighted by learned feature directions. The paper proposes a theoretical framework (Approximate Feature Activation, AFA) that approximates the ℓ2-norm of sparse feature activations from input embeddings under the linear representation hypothesis. The core mechanism involves: (1) encoder mapping inputs to sparse latents via an activation function, (2) decoder reconstructing from sparse features, (3) a norm-matching loss that aligns sparse activation norms with input embedding norms, and (4) a novel top-AFA activation function that adaptively selects the number of active features per input by matching norms, eliminating the need for manually tuned sparsity parameter k.
trained_components: top-AFA activation function parameters (implicit in norm-matching logic)

biblio.title: Evaluating and Designing Sparse Autoencoders by Approximating Quasi-Orthogonality

claims.statement: The ℓ2-norm of sparse feature vector can be approximated with the ℓ2-norm of dense vector with a closed-form error bound that depends on the quasi-orthogonality of the dictionary and the dimensionality of the feature space.

baseline.name: top-k SAE and batch top-k SAE

claims.statement: Top-AFA SAE achieves better reconstruction performance than top-k and batch top-k SAEs, surpassing the scaling law boundary reported by Gao et al. (2024).

claims.dataset: GPT-2 Small and Gemma Scope 2B; 1k sequences of length 128 from OpenWebText
claims.statement: The proposed AFA-based evaluation metric (LBO, lower bound of quasi-orthogonality) provides a different perspective on SAE quality compared to normalized mean squared error (NMSE), yielding different distributional shapes, modality, and ranking order across layers.
Top-AFA activation function enables top-k style activation without requiring constant hyperparameter k to be tuned, dynamically determining the number of activated features for each input.
Pre-trained SAE decoders (GPT-2 Small, Gemma Scope) often fail to achieve the theoretically permitted level of quasi-orthogonality predicted by the Johnson-Lindenstrauss lemma.

The ZF Plot visualization demonstrates that existing unsupervised SAE evaluation metrics assess either reconstruction quality (relationship between x and x̂) or sparsity of f in isolation, overlooking the input-driven alignment between embeddings and feature activations.

Top-AFA introduces a new hyperparameter λ_AFA (a training-time regularization coefficient) that requires tuning; a stable coefficient value of 1/16 was observed across all tested layers but the necessity of this hyperparameter remains an open question.
The theoretical analysis is limited to the linear representation hypothesis; extending AFA to more general superposition settings (e.g., two-layer decoders, Matryoshka SAEs) remains an open question.
